title: Stack & Queue
summary: Model ordered, sequential processing — stacks for last-in-first-out (undo/redo), queues for first-in-first-out (event pipelines).
overview: A stack adds and removes from the same end (LIFO). A queue adds at one end and removes from the other (FIFO). Both are abstract interfaces with concrete use cases: stacks model call stacks, undo history, and bracket matching; queues model task queues, BFS, and event streams.
coreConcept: Stack: push to add, pop to remove, peek to inspect top — all O(1). Queue: enqueue at rear, dequeue from front — O(1) with a proper deque or circular buffer. In JavaScript, arrays serve as both but array.shift() is O(n); use an index pointer for O(1) dequeuing.
signals: You need to reverse order or backtrack
Last action needs to be undone

frontendScenarios.title: Undo/Redo in a Rich Text Editor
frontendScenarios.description: Two stacks: undoStack and redoStack. Every edit pushes state to undoStack. Undo pops from undoStack and pushes to redoStack. Redo pops from redoStack and pushes back to undoStack.

frontendScenarios.title: Router History (Back/Forward)
frontendScenarios.description: Browser history is modeled as a stack of pages visited. pushState adds to the stack. Back pops the current page and moves to the previous. Forward requires a second stack.

dataThinking: Stacks are implicit in recursive code — each function call pushes a frame, return pops it. When you see recursive algorithms, think 'could I make the stack explicit?' This is essential for iterative tree traversal and avoiding stack overflows in deep nesting scenarios common in component trees.

naiveApproach.description: Use a plain array and track state manually without clear push/pop semantics. Often leads to off-by-one errors.

betterApproach.description: Encapsulate the stack/queue logic in a class or custom hook. Use a pointer-based queue for O(1) dequeue instead of array.shift().

visualizationSteps.label: Undo stack after 3 edits
visualizationSteps.explanation: User made 3 edits. Each push adds to undoStack. Current state is the top: 'edit3'.

visualizationSteps.label: After undo
visualizationSteps.explanation: Undo: pop 'edit3' from undoStack, push to redoStack. Current is now 'edit2'.

visualizationSteps.label: After new edit — redo cleared
visualizationSteps.explanation: New edit while redoStack had items: clear redoStack (can't redo past a branch), push new state.

practiceItems.title: Valid Parentheses in JSX
practiceItems.question:
JSX uses nested tags like brackets. Write a validator that checks if a simplified tag string like '
' has properly nested and closed tags.
practiceItems.hint1: Use a stack. Push opening tags, pop and verify when you see a closing tag.
practiceItems.hint2: Extract tag names from '<tagName>' and '</tagName>' patterns using string parsing.

practiceItems.title: Implement Browser History
practiceItems.question: Implement a RouterHistory class with: navigate(url), back(), forward(), and current property. Navigating to a new page should clear the forward stack.
practiceItems.hint1: Two stacks: backStack (pages you can go back to) and forwardStack (pages you can redo).
practiceItems.hint2: current is always the top of backStack. navigate() pushes to backStack and clears forwardStack.

reflectionPrompts: The JavaScript call stack is literally a stack. How does this affect how you think about recursion depth?
Why does array.shift() perform poorly as a queue operation? What data structure would you use in a performance-critical queue?
Undo/redo systems use two stacks. How would you extend this to support branching history (like git)?
Event loops process tasks from a queue. How does the microtask queue vs macrotask queue difference relate to FIFO ordering?
